title: 🎙️ Indigenous Lands & Innovation
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Indigenous Lands & Innovation' — Indigenous nations using technology, governance, and stewardship on their own terms.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Indigenous-led journalism on governance, rights, and self-determination should score in the moderate-to-good range even without an explicit technology angle — do not collapse everything that lacks a 'tech' hook to near 0:
- 70-100: Cariboo-specific stories bridging Indigenous governance/culture AND technology — Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh language revitalization tech, guardian programs using sensors/drones, GIS land mapping, Indigenous-led connectivity, digital cultural heritage projects.
- 45-69: Pan-Canadian Indigenous governance, sovereignty, land rights, treaty, or reconciliation journalism — APTN, Eagle Feather News, or IndigiNews-style coverage of First Nations governance, MMIWG, land claims, self-determination, or Indigenous-led services — even without a technology angle, per the curator's standing interest in Indigenous governance and sovereignty.
- 25-44: Indigenous cultural, community, or human-interest stories (awards, events, ceremonies) with limited governance or tech substance, or BC land-stewardship/environmental stories with meaningful First Nations involvement.
- 10-24: General BC land, environmental, or forestry stories that mention Indigenous communities only in passing.
- 0-9: No Indigenous angle at all.

Strong fits include: language revitalization technology for Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh, Indigenous guardian programs using remote monitoring and sensors, land management tech and environmental monitoring on Indigenous lands, digital tools for traditional knowledge preservation, community mapping and GIS for land claims, Indigenous-led connectivity projects, technology for cultural heritage documentation, mobile apps for language learning, drone/satellite monitoring for stewardship, reconciliation through digital collaboration, how Cariboo Indigenous nations use innovation for self-determination, and Indigenous governance, sovereignty, treaty, and land-rights journalism from any Canadian nation. Prefer articles that bridge Indigenous governance/culture AND technology, but governance/sovereignty journalism alone is a solid fit. Penalise heavily (score 0–9): international geopolitical and war news with no Indigenous rights angle; general wildfire and BC Wildlife Service updates not led by or directly impacting Indigenous communities or guardians; provincial forestry and environmental policy without explicit First Nations involvement; consumer tech and gadget reviews; science discoveries without Indigenous stewardship or sovereignty context; crime and judicial news unrelated to Indigenous rights. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] ‘It’s open green space versus construction’: Delhi’s historic polo club and the battle for the city’s future
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

The government’s requisition of a historic green space has ignited a fierce debate about air quality and heat stress in India’s scorching capital

For decades, the social highlight of winters in Delhi for the “beautiful people” was the polo season. A sprinkling of royalty and diplomats, impeccably groomed women in pearls and chiffon saris, along with wealthy industrialists sporting silk pocket squares used to gather to watch polo players compete under the mild, balmy sun.

They cheered on handsome players who, once the match was over, had children shrieking in delight as they put on a heart-stopping display of tent-pegging derring-do. Swish champagne lunches and other après-polo celebrations followed.

Continue reading...

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Trafficked, beaten and raped: raids reveal scale of abuse of women in Asia’s cyberscam centres
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

As tens of thousands are freed, female survivors are increasingly reporting gender-based violence in the compounds, previously thought to hold mainly men

Late one evening in October 2023, Sarah* felt labour pains starting. It was 11pm, but at the cyberscam compound inside Laos’ Golden Triangle, workers were logging on for a long night shift, scamming Americans online.

Every night, workers sat at their computers until the early hours, building fake profiles of glamorous, jet-setting women on Facebook and Instagram. Sarah trawled the web to find older men to target with messages, where she fawned over their jobs, asked how their day had been and exchanged photos of luxury travel and beach trips. Each conversation she had was meticulously designed to follow a multi-day script, and monitored by bosses who walked up and down the long rows of desks.

Continue reading...

It was 11pm, but at the cyberscam compound inside Laos’ Golden Triangle,

items.content_html:

Transit 2 min.read # Summer traffic in the Kootenays increases risk for roadside workers My East Kootenay Now Staff By My East Kootenay Now Staff June 28, 2026 FacebookXReddItWhatsAppEmail With the July 1 holiday marking the start of the busy summer travel season, drivers across the Kootenays will encounter more orange cones, construction crews and roadside workers as highways and local roads see increased traffic. items.content_html:

## Judge denies jury trial bid for Kelowna man accused of sexual assault Published 3:30 pm Friday, June 26, 2026 **By Brittany Webster** Brandon Hildebrand Acosta Brandon Hildebrand Acosta A Kelowna man has lost his right to be tried by a jury of his peers after he failed to show up to court. Brandon Hildebrand Acosta appeared in person in the B.C. Supreme Court in Kelowna to file an application to retain his right to be tried by both a judge and a jury after he did not show up at the start of his jury trial. Hildebrand Acosta was scheduled to appear for the selection of a jury and the start

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Astronomers found two rare super puff planets lighter than cotton candy
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Two newly confirmed "super-puff" planets are so diffuse that they are less dense than cotton candy, despite being about the size of Jupiter. Their rare orbital relationship and enormous, lightweight atmospheres could provide valuable clues about how some of the strangest planets in the galaxy come to exist.

# Ernest “Ernie” Manchur 1 Published June 30, 2026 **May 12, 1935 – June 15, 2026** It is with sadness that we announce the passing of Ernest “Ernie” Manchur in June 2026 at the age of 91. Ernie was a devoted husband, father, grandfather, and great-grandfather whose life was defined by hard work, dedication to family, and a love of the outdoors. He is survived by his beloved wife, Harriet, with whom he shared 68 wonderful years of marriage. items.title: [ScienceDaily] Massive study links teen marijuana use to double the risk of serious mental illness
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Teens who use cannabis may face a substantially greater risk of developing serious mental health conditions, including psychotic and bipolar disorders, according to a study of more than 463,000 adolescents. Researchers found cannabis use often preceded these diagnoses by nearly two years, strengthening concerns about its long-term effects on developing brains.

Researchers found cannabis use often preceded these diagnoses by nearly two years,
